chunks.title: Fever Self-Care and OTC Use in Adults
chunks.chunk_text: For mild fever, rest, hydration, and light meals are first-line self-care. Paracetamol is commonly used for fever discomfort. Avoid duplicate products that contain the same active ingredient. In older adults, prolonged fever, confusion, poor fluid intake, or weakness should trigger same-day clinician contact.
chunks.otc_guidance: Paracetamol is commonly used for fever discomfort in adults.
chunks.citation_snippet: Hydration and symptom control are first-line; escalating signs require clinical review.

chunks.title: Cough and Cold Symptom Relief
chunks.chunk_text: Most uncomplicated cough and cold symptoms can be managed with fluids, rest, and targeted OTC symptom relief. Product choice should match the main symptom such as cough, sore throat, or congestion. Seek urgent care for breathing difficulty, chest pain, or confusion.
chunks.otc_guidance: Choose OTC products by dominant symptom rather than multiple overlapping products.
contraindications: Use caution with sedating products in older adults
chunks.citation_snippet: Symptom-targeted treatment and red-flag monitoring reduce avoidable risk.

chunks.title: Mild-to-Moderate Pain OTC Options
chunks.chunk_text: For mild pain, common OTC choices include paracetamol and ibuprofen when clinically appropriate. In older adults and people with kidney disease, stomach ulcer history, or anticoagulant use, NSAID risk may be higher. If pain is severe, persistent, or function-limiting, clinical review is needed.
chunks.otc_guidance: Paracetamol is often preferred first in many older adults; NSAID suitability varies by comorbidity.
red_flags: Trauma with inability to bear weight
chunks.citation_snippet: Analgesic choice should account for comorbidity and medicine interactions.

chunks.title: Stomach Upset, Nausea, and Early Home Care
chunks.chunk_text: For mild stomach upset, small frequent fluids and gentle meals are helpful. Watch for dehydration signs such as dry mouth, reduced urination, dizziness, or confusion. Older adults can dehydrate quickly and may need earlier in-person assessment.
chunks.otc_guidance: Focus first on hydration and symptom tracking before adding multiple OTC products.
contraindications: Avoid unnecessary anti-diarrheal use with fever or blood in stool
chunks.citation_snippet: Hydration and early warning recognition are central in GI symptom management.

chunks.title: Diabetes Safety in Daily Symptom Changes
chunks.chunk_text: For people with diabetes, illness can destabilize glucose control. Keep hydration, continue monitoring, and seek care if repeated low sugar episodes, persistent high readings, confusion, or inability to keep fluids occur.
chunks.otc_guidance: Confirm OTC selection with pharmacist to avoid glucose-impacting combinations.
contraindications: Review interaction risk with existing diabetes medications
chunks.citation_snippet: Intercurrent illness in diabetes requires closer monitoring and earlier escalation thresholds.

chunks.title: Blood Pressure Conditions and Symptom Triage
chunks.chunk_text: In people with hypertension, headache alone is not always an emergency, but severe headache with chest pain, neurologic symptoms, shortness of breath, or confusion needs urgent evaluation. Medicine adherence and blood pressure log review are key.
chunks.otc_guidance: Ask pharmacist before decongestants if you have blood pressure conditions.
contraindications: Some cold/decongestant products can raise blood pressure
chunks.citation_snippet: Comorbidity-aware symptom triage is essential in hypertension management.

chunks.title: Polypharmacy and Interaction Risk for Older Adults
chunks.chunk_text: Older adults often use multiple medicines and face higher interaction risk. New OTC products should be screened against current medication lists. Sedation, dizziness, and confusion increase fall risk.
chunks.otc_guidance: Bring your full medication list to a pharmacist before starting a new OTC medicine.
chunks.citation_snippet: Medication reconciliation reduces preventable harm in older adults.

chunks.title: Falls and Mobility-Related Injury Triage
chunks.chunk_text: After a fall, assess pain, head impact, ability to stand, and confusion. Older adults on blood thinners need lower thresholds for urgent evaluation after head injury. Recurrent falls should trigger medication and home-safety review.
chunks.otc_guidance: Pain self-care may be reasonable for minor injuries, but confirm safety with pharmacist when on chronic medications.
contraindications: Head impact on anticoagulants needs urgent review
chunks.citation_snippet: Fall-related risk stratification is critical in older adults, especially with anticoagulant use.

chunks.title: Urinary Symptoms in Older Adults
chunks.chunk_text: Urinary pain, frequency, or urgency can indicate infection. In older adults, confusion or sudden functional decline may be atypical warning signs and should prompt earlier clinician review.
chunks.otc_guidance: Hydration and symptom monitoring are first steps while arranging pharmacist or clinician advice.
contraindications: Do not rely only on OTC symptom suppression when red flags are present
chunks.citation_snippet: Older-adult presentations of infection may be atypical and require lower escalation thresholds.

chunks.title: Constipation Management in Midlife and Older Age
chunks.chunk_text: For constipation, increase fluids, fiber as tolerated, and movement where safe. Review medications that can worsen constipation. Persistent symptoms, severe pain, vomiting, or blood warrant clinician review.
chunks.otc_guidance: Bulk-forming or osmotic options may help, but suitability varies with fluid intake and chronic conditions.
red_flags: No bowel movement with worsening symptoms
chunks.citation_snippet: Conservative management is first-line, but red flags require prompt clinical assessment.

chunks.title: Sleep Problems and Anxiety-Related Physical Symptoms
chunks.chunk_text: Sleep disturbance and anxiety can amplify pain and physical discomfort. Initial support includes sleep hygiene and stress reduction strategies. Sedating OTC products should be used cautiously in older adults due to fall and confusion risk.
chunks.otc_guidance: Prefer non-sedating strategies first and confirm sedative safety with pharmacist.
contraindications: Sedative use caution in older adults
chunks.citation_snippet: Non-pharmacologic approaches often reduce risk in sleep/anxiety symptom management.

chunks.title: Rash and Minor Skin Symptom Guidance
chunks.chunk_text: Mild localized rash may be monitored with gentle skin care and trigger avoidance. Widespread rash with breathing issues, facial swelling, fever, or rapid worsening needs urgent assessment.
chunks.otc_guidance: Mild topical symptom relief may help when no red flags are present.
contraindications: Avoid prolonged topical steroid self-use without review
chunks.citation_snippet: Escalation decisions should prioritize systemic and rapidly progressive signs.

chunks.title: Chest Pain and Emergency Escalation
chunks.chunk_text: Chest pain with shortness of breath, sweating, nausea, fainting, or radiation to arm/jaw is an emergency pattern. Do not delay urgent services. AI guidance should prioritize immediate escalation and human oversight.
chunks.otc_guidance: Do not self-manage possible cardiac emergency with OTC medication.
chunks.citation_snippet: Cardiac-pattern chest symptoms require immediate emergency escalation.

chunks.title: Stroke Warning Signs and Immediate Action
chunks.chunk_text: Facial droop, arm weakness, speech disturbance, or sudden confusion suggest possible stroke and require immediate emergency response. Time-sensitive evaluation is critical.
chunks.otc_guidance: Do not delay emergency care for possible stroke by trying OTC medicines.
contraindications: No self-treatment delay for stroke signs
chunks.citation_snippet: Suspected stroke is a time-critical emergency requiring immediate escalation.

chunks.title: Breathing Difficulty Severity Screening
chunks.chunk_text: New or worsening breathing difficulty, especially at rest, with chest pain, cyanosis, or confusion is urgent. Rapid deterioration risk is higher in frail older adults and those with chronic lung or heart conditions.
chunks.otc_guidance: OTC symptom treatment should not delay urgent assessment for breathing difficulty.
contraindications: No delay in urgent care for respiratory distress
chunks.citation_snippet: Respiratory distress patterns should trigger immediate escalation pathways.

chunks.title: Caregiver Checklist for Older Adult Symptom Calls
chunks.issuer: WHO integrated care for older people (prototype-adapted)
chunks.chunk_text: Caregivers should track symptom onset, baseline function changes, medication list, allergies, fluid intake, and mobility changes. This improves triage safety and handoff quality.
chunks.otc_guidance: Confirm medicine changes with pharmacist and keep one up-to-date medication list.
contraindications: Avoid unsupervised medication changes when cognition is impaired
chunks.citation_snippet: Structured caregiver observations materially improve decision quality in older-adult care.

chunks.title: Prevention and Seasonal Infection Preparedness
chunks.chunk_text: Prevention for older adults includes vaccination updates, hand hygiene, ventilation, and early symptom recognition. Timely testing and early consultation may reduce complications in high-risk groups.
chunks.otc_guidance: Home symptom kits can help early management but should not replace clinical review for red flags.
contraindications: Do not rely only on self-treatment in high-risk older adults with worsening symptoms
chunks.citation_snippet: Prevention plus early escalation lowers severe outcomes in vulnerable populations.

chunks.title: Medication Review Timing and Escalation
chunks.chunk_text: Medication review is recommended after any new symptom pattern, recent hospital discharge, or recurrent side effects. Older adults benefit from periodic pharmacist-led reconciliation to reduce duplication and interaction risk.
chunks.otc_guidance: Consult a pharmacist before adding any new OTC medicine to an existing regimen.
contraindications: Avoid abrupt stopping of chronic prescriptions without clinician advice
red_flags: New confusion or falls after medication changes
chunks.citation_snippet: Routine medication reconciliation is a high-value safety step for older adults.
